panels.title: TMM CPU utilisation % (cycles-based, stacked by node)
TMM client throughput (bit/s, software path only)
panels.description: NOTE: on a DOCA/DPDK hardware-offloaded datapath (BlueField), established flows are handled in hardware and never reach TMM's software byte counters — so this UNDER-REPORTS real traffic by orders of magnitude (measured: 92 KB counted while ~39 GB actually moved). Verified equal to tmctl, so it is TMM's accounting, not the reader. Use it for shape/attribution, not absolute throughput; for real bytes read the DPU's OVS/DOCA flow counters (ovs-appctl dpctl/dump-flows).

panels.title: Interface throughput in (bit/s, software path only)
panels.description: NOTE: on a DOCA/DPDK hardware-offloaded datapath (BlueField), established flows are handled in hardware and never reach TMM's software byte counters — so this UNDER-REPORTS real traffic by orders of magnitude (measured: 92 KB counted while ~39 GB actually moved). Verified equal to tmctl, so it is TMM's accounting, not the reader. Use it for shape/attribution, not absolute throughput; for real bytes read the DPU's OVS/DOCA flow counters (ovs-appctl dpctl/dump-flows).

panels.title: Virtual-server connection rate (new conns/s)
panels.description: Connections per second, NOT requests: with HTTP keep-alive one connection carries many requests (a 289k req/s bench shows ~7 conn/s across 128 connections). For request rate, use the load generator.

panels.title: Per-tenant connection rate (new conns/s)
panels.description: Connections per second, NOT requests: with HTTP keep-alive one connection carries many requests (a 289k req/s bench shows ~7 conn/s across 128 connections). For request rate, use the load generator. Tenant parsed from the VS name, which embeds it.

panels.title: Per-tenant client throughput (bit/s, software path only)
panels.description: NOTE: on a DOCA/DPDK hardware-offloaded datapath (BlueField), established flows are handled in hardware and never reach TMM's software byte counters — so this UNDER-REPORTS real traffic by orders of magnitude (measured: 92 KB counted while ~39 GB actually moved). Verified equal to tmctl, so it is TMM's accounting, not the reader. Use it for shape/attribution, not absolute throughput; for real bytes read the DPU's OVS/DOCA flow counters (ovs-appctl dpctl/dump-flows).

panels.title: Pool-member current connections (per tenant)
panels.description: Live server-side connections per origin. Accurate — connection state is TMM's, not offloaded.

panels.title: Pool-member connection rate (load-balance check)
panels.description: New server-side connections/s per origin — should split evenly across each tenant's two origins; divergence means LB skew. Connection counts ARE accurate (setup goes through TMM).

panels.title: SF (DPDK) receive rate — per service function
panels.description: From the tmm_xnet segment. Counts the SF slow path only; offloaded flows bypass it. NOTE: on a DOCA/DPDK hardware-offloaded datapath (BlueField), established flows are handled in hardware and never reach TMM's software byte counters — so this UNDER-REPORTS real traffic by orders of magnitude (measured: 92 KB counted while ~39 GB actually moved). Verified equal to tmctl, so it is TMM's accounting, not the reader. Use it for shape/attribution, not absolute throughput; for real bytes read the DPU's OVS/DOCA flow counters (ovs-appctl dpctl/dump-flows).

panels.title: SF ring drops & errors
panels.description: Non-zero here means the SF ring is overrunning — the first thing to check on a throughput regression.

panels.title: TMM interface throughput (bit/s, in+out, software path only)
panels.description: Dataplane SFs are 1.1 / 1.2; 0.1 is management and tap:31 the kernel tap. NOTE: on a DOCA/DPDK hardware-offloaded datapath (BlueField), established flows are handled in hardware and never reach TMM's software byte counters — so this UNDER-REPORTS real traffic by orders of magnitude (measured: 92 KB counted while ~39 GB actually moved). Verified equal to tmctl, so it is TMM's accounting, not the reader. Use it for shape/attribution, not absolute throughput; for real bytes read the DPU's OVS/DOCA flow counters (ovs-appctl dpctl/dump-flows).

panels.title: Real dataplane throughput per tenant (bit/s, hardware-offloaded)
panels.description: Filtered to the HOST-facing ports (pf0hpf/pf1hpf) — the leg carrying the full proxied flow. Validated against the load generator: an 83.8 s sustained run measured 5.83 GB/s = 46.6 Gbit/s client-side, and this panel read a 47.04 Gbit/s median. Do NOT sum across all ports: a packet is counted at every port it traverses (measured 501 GB across all ports vs 103 GB on the fabric ports for one bench), and the fabric ports (p0/p1) see only one leg, reading ~2x low. OVS refreshes hardware flow counters on its ~5 s revalidator cadence, so bursts shorter than the rate window read low — use sustained load for true plateaus. Rendering: the rate window is FIXED at 15 s inside a max_over_time subquery, deliberately not $__rate_interval. $__rate_interval grows with the dashboard time range, and a wide window turns a square load pulse into a slow ramp and smears one tenant's tail across the gap into the next tenant's phase — both artifacts, not traffic. The underlying counter is a clean square wave: measured per 5 s, acme ran 0,0,24.3,34.5,33.9,34.4,34.3,34.5,34.4,33.8,28.6,0.4,0 GB while bravo held exactly 0.0 throughout, then pulsed on its own. With this expression the shape and the amplitude survive zooming out (55 Gbit/s at a 15 s step AND at a 60 s step, vs ~20 with a 2 m window). Each point is the PEAK 15 s rate within its bucket, not the mean — chosen so zooming out never hides a burst. Linear interpolation on purpose: spline smoothing draws curvature that is not in the data.

panels.title: Real dataplane packet rate per tenant (host-facing ports)
panels.description: Filtered to the HOST-facing ports (pf0hpf/pf1hpf) — the leg carrying the full proxied flow. Validated against the load generator: an 83.8 s sustained run measured 5.83 GB/s = 46.6 Gbit/s client-side, and this panel read a 47.04 Gbit/s median. Do NOT sum across all ports: a packet is counted at every port it traverses (measured 501 GB across all ports vs 103 GB on the fabric ports for one bench), and the fabric ports (p0/p1) see only one leg, reading ~2x low. OVS refreshes hardware flow counters on its ~5 s revalidator cadence, so bursts shorter than the rate window read low — use sustained load for true plateaus. With this expression the shape and the amplitude survive zooming out (55 Gbit/s at a 15 s step AND at a 60 s step, vs ~20 with a 2 m window). Each point is the PEAK 15 s rate within its bucket, not the mean — chosen so zooming out never hides a burst. Linear interpolation on purpose: spline smoothing draws curvature that is not in the data.

panels.title: Dataplane throughput by port (bit/s)
panels.description: Per-port view: p0/p1 are the fabric uplinks, pf0hpf/pf1hpf face the host, en3f0pf0sfNNN are the service functions. Useful for seeing where a packet is spending its hops. Rendering: the rate window is FIXED at 15 s inside a max_over_time subquery, deliberately not $__rate_interval. $__rate_interval grows with the dashboard time range, and a wide window turns a square load pulse into a slow ramp and smears one tenant's tail across the gap into the next tenant's phase — both artifacts, not traffic. The underlying counter is a clean square wave: measured per 5 s, acme ran 0,0,24.3,34.5,33.9,34.4,34.3,34.5,34.4,33.8,28.6,0.4,0 GB while bravo held exactly 0.0 throughout, then pulsed on its own. With this expression the shape and the amplitude survive zooming out (55 Gbit/s at a 15 s step AND at a 60 s step, vs ~20 with a 2 m window). Each point is the PEAK 15 s rate within its bucket, not the mean — chosen so zooming out never hides a burst. Linear interpolation on purpose: spline smoothing draws curvature that is not in the data.

panels.title: OVS datapath flows (offloaded vs total)
panels.description: If offloaded drops below total, flows are falling back to software — the datapath is about to get much slower.
